skill.name: todoist-automation
skill.description: Automate Todoist task, project, section, filter, and bulk workflows through Rube MCP and Todoist OAuth.
skill.summary: Use Rube MCP to manage Todoist tasks, projects, sections, filters, and bulk task workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows they are Markdown backtick references to Todoist and Rube MCP tool names, not executable Ruby or shell code. The main confirmed risk is expected network access through Rube MCP and Todoist OAuth, with possible destructive Todoist operations if the user approves them. No prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration logic, or embedded executable script was found.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party MCP and Todoist OAuth Access
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill instructs users to connect Rube MCP and activate Todoist OAuth. This is expected for the skill, but it gives a third-party integration access to task data and task-changing operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly require Rube MCP and Todoist OAuth, then document task creation, completion, deletion, section deletion, and other account changes. The behavior is legitimate for a Todoist automation skill, but it is privacy-sensitive and can be destructive.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded External MCP Endpoint
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill directs users to add https://rube.app/mcp as an MCP server. This network dependency is required functionality, not hidden exfiltration, but users should understand the external service boundary.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is plainly documented as the Rube MCP endpoint and aligns with the stated Todoist automation purpose. No evidence found that it sends unrelated secrets or hidden data.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Tool References Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The many external_commands detections are inline Markdown code spans naming Rube and Todoist tools or parameters. They are documentation references, not Ruby backtick execution or shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation and the flagged text appears inside inline code spans or tables. No executable Ruby file, shell script, command substitution, or user-input command path is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts occur in prose and parameter descriptions. No hashing, encryption, crypto library usage, or credential handling code appears in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe Todoist fields such as description, content, and task objects. I found no crypto implementation or weak algorithm invocation in the file.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Todoist Lookup Guidance
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The reconnaissance alerts map to instructions for listing Todoist sections, checking IDs, or spacing API requests. They do not inspect the host system, filesystem, network interfaces, or environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is Todoist API workflow documentation. No evidence found of host reconnaissance commands or collection of local system information.

content.value_statement: Manual Todoist cleanup and planning can be slow across projects, sections, and filters. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through reliable Todoist actions using Rube MCP.
actual_capabilities: Connects through Rube MCP and checks Todoist OAuth connection status before workflows.
Creates, updates, completes, reopens, and deletes Todoist tasks using documented tool sequences.
Lists, creates, and updates Todoist projects with colors, view styles, favorites, and parent projects.
Creates, updates, and deletes sections after resolving the correct project and section identifiers.
Searches active and completed tasks with Todoist filter syntax, date ranges, labels, and pagination guidance.
Builds multiple tasks in one bulk request while preserving project, section, due date, label, and priority details.
limitations: Requires a working Rube MCP server and an active Todoist OAuth connection.
Cannot operate without the current Todoist tool schemas returned by RUBE_SEARCH_TOOLS.
Todoist API limits, filter syntax rules, and ID format differences can still cause request failures.
Destructive actions such as deleting tasks or sections should be confirmed by the user before execution.

use_cases.title: Plan a Project Launch
use_cases.description: Create projects, sections, and batches of dated tasks for a launch plan in Todoist.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Task Lists
use_cases.description: Find overdue, duplicate, or misplaced tasks and move them into the right projects and sections.

use_cases.title: Review Completed Work
use_cases.description: Retrieve completed tasks for a date range and summarize progress by project or section.

prompt_templates.title: Create One Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Todoist task called "Send weekly update" in my Inbox due tomorrow at 3pm with normal priority.

prompt_templates.title: Organize a Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Todoist project named "Website Refresh" with sections for Planning, Design, Build, Review, and Launch.

prompt_templates.title: Find and Update Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Find all overdue tasks in my Work project, show them to me, then update the approved ones with new due dates.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Full Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Todoist launch workflow with project sections, milestone tasks, labels, priorities, durations, and recurring review reminders.

output_examples.input: Create three onboarding tasks for a new hire starting next Monday.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the target project, confirms the Todoist connection, and creates dated onboarding tasks with clear titles and labels.

output_examples.input: Show completed tasks for the last month in the Marketing project.
output_examples.output: The assistant retrieves completed tasks within the supported date range and summarizes completion counts, notable tasks, and remaining follow-up work.

output_examples.input: Move planning tasks into a new section for my product roadmap.
output_examples.output: The assistant resolves the project and section IDs, confirms the changes, then updates the matching tasks after user approval.

best_practices: Call RUBE_SEARCH_TOOLS before each workflow so the assistant uses current Todoist tool schemas.
Resolve project, section, and task names to API IDs before making changes.
Confirm destructive actions and large bulk edits before sending Todoist requests.
anti_patterns: Do not place due dates or durations inside task titles when Todoist fields are available.
Do not assume Todoist UI priority labels match API priority numbers.
Do not use completed-task filter terms with the incomplete-task endpoint.

faq.question: Does this skill require Todoist OAuth?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses Rube MCP and requires an active Todoist connection before it can manage tasks.

faq.question: Can it create many tasks at once?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents the bulk task creation workflow and the fields supported for each task.

faq.question: Can it find completed tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses the completed-task date range tool and explains pagination and range limits.

faq.question: Does it store Todoist data locally?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The skill is a Markdown guide and does not include local storage code.

faq.question: Can it delete tasks or sections?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents delete tools, so assistants should ask for confirmation before destructive actions.

faq.question: Why does it search tools first?
faq.answer: Rube MCP tool schemas can change, so the skill instructs assistants to fetch current schemas before use.
